title: An Asymptotic Theory of Chain-of-Thought in In-Context Learning
abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has become a widely used mechanism for eliciting multi-step reasoning in large language models by generating intermediate reasoning steps at inference time. Yet the scaling behavior of generalization with CoT depth remains poorly understood. To address this question, we study a theoretically solvable model of CoT for in-context weight prediction in linear regression, where test-time reasoning is represented as an iterative refinement of the weight-parameter estimate. Using tools from random matrix theory under high-dimensional asymptotics, we derive an exact formula for the generalization error as a function of reasoning depth, pretraining data amount, and context length. Our analysis reveals a sharp phase transition separating exponential and polynomial improvement, saturation, and overthinking, and characterizes how the optimal reasoning depth scales. We further show that deeper reasoning is most effective with sufficiently rich pretraining and in-context information, whereas limited pretraining or context makes longer reasoning prone to error amplification or saturation. We also validate these predictions through experiments on fully learned linear attention and softmax attention models. Our results provide a unified theoretical account of how test-time CoT depth affects generalization.
summary: This paper provides a deep theoretical understanding of how the depth of Chain-of-Thought reasoning affects model generalization in In-Context Learning. Using random matrix theory on a simplified linear model, the authors derive an exact formula that predicts four distinct regimes of performance scaling. This work clarifies that the benefit of deep reasoning is highly dependent on the quality of pretraining and the amount of in-context examples provided.
main_problem: The paper addresses the poorly understood scaling behavior of generalization error when using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning within In-Context Learning (ICL) for large language models.
main_result: It derives an exact asymptotic formula characterizing four distinct regimes of test-time error dynamics: exponential improvement, polynomial decay, saturation, and error amplification (overthinking).
method: The analysis employs tools from random matrix theory under high-dimensional asymptotics, modeling the CoT process as an iterative refinement of a weight-parameter estimate in a solvable linear regression model.
model_or_system: The system is a theoretically solvable model of CoT applied to in-context weight prediction in linear regression, analyzed via an attention mechanism structure. The analysis tracks the error dynamics as an iterative update $\hat{w}_{t+1} := 	ext{Read}(	ext{Atten}(P_t; 	heta^*))$.
key_observables: Generalization error (MSE) as a function of reasoning depth ($t$), pretraining data amount, and context length ($\alpha$).
important_parameters: Reasoning depth ($t$), context length ($\alpha$), task diversity ($	au$), and noise variance ($\sigma^2$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a theoretically tractable model (linear regression) and the high-dimensional asymptotic limit ($D, M, L 	o \infty$).
figures_summary: The figures summarize a phase diagram showing the four distinct regimes (exponential improvement, polynomial decay, saturation, overthinking) based on the relative values of task diversity ($	au$) versus a critical threshold ($	au_c$).
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the iterative error dynamics using random matrix theory, deriving the asymptotic scaling law, identifying the phase transition points, and finally characterizing the optimal stopping depth ($t^*$) in the overthinking regime.
why_interesting: The rigorous application of random matrix theory and asymptotic analysis to model the generalization dynamics of LLMs provides a powerful theoretical bridge between statistical physics/random matrix theory and modern deep learning theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Phase diagram of the test-time scaling law and representative error dynamics. (A) Heatmap of theoretical prediction of the generalization error (MSE) at a fixed test-time depth t = 80. The diagram is divided into four regimes: (I) the overthinking regime, τ < τc(α, σ2), where long test- time CoT amplifies the error; (II) the polynomial-decay regime, α > 1 and τ = τc(α, σ2), where the error decreases only polynomially; (III) the saturation regime, 0 < α < 1 and τ > τc(α, σ2), where the dynamics is stable but the error converges to a nonzero limit; and (IV) the exponential-decay regime, α > 1 and τ > τc(α, σ2), where the error decays exponentially to zero. (B,C) Theoretical...

Figure 2: CoT experiments in the fully learned linear attention and softmax attention models. (A, C) Phase diagrams of the test-time generalization error at t = 20 as a function of the context length L and the number of training tasks M, for (A) the fully learned linear attention model and (C) the softmax attention model, respectively. (B, D) Test-time generalization error as a function of reasoning depth in the (B) fully learned linear attention model and (D) the softmax attention model, respectively. The solid curves correspond to an exponential decay regime, the dashed curves to an overthinking regime, and the dotted curves to a saturation regime. Parameters: (A-D) λ = 10−5, σ2 = 0, and...
Figure 3: Heatmaps of the pretrained full-parameter matrices W and V. Parameters: (A,B) λ = 10−3, σ = 0.1 (A) M = N = 20, (B) M = N = 200.

Figure 4: Comparison between the theoretical prediction and numerical experiments. The solid lines show the theoretical prediction in the D →∞limit, while scatter points show numerical results at finite D. Parameters: (A-D) λ = 10−5, σ = 0.01. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean over 5 trials per point.
